pattern: Sliding a fixed-size window one step only removes one element's inversions with the rest of the window and adds one new element's inversions with what remains, so a value-indexed counting structure lets the whole scan update in logarithmic time per step instead of recomputing from scratch.
hints: Compute the inversion count of the very first window directly, inserting elements one at a time and, for each new element, counting how many already-inserted elements are strictly greater than it.

When the window slides by one, exactly two things change: the leaving (leftmost) element stops contributing its inversions with everyone else in the old window, and the entering (new rightmost) element starts contributing inversions with everyone still in the window.

A value-indexed Fenwick tree (built over the compressed set of array values) answers 'how many currently-in-window elements are less than / greater than this value' in logarithmic time, which is exactly what both the removal and insertion steps need.

edge_inputs.description: statement example 2, only one window, fully reversed
statement example 3, k = 1 always has zero inversions
an already-sorted array has a window with zero inversions
duplicate values are not inversions with each other
strictly decreasing then strictly increasing, best window straddles the valley

stress.description: large random array with a random fixed window length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [nums, k]
